summary: Phase 2 mission text read; starting by recording Mission Update 002 and clearing the two resolved approvals, then building the shared dataset harness and running the two small BEIR baselines.

Mission Update 002 and Mission Update 003 are recorded in the mission updates file, the two resolved approvals are cleared from project state, the Phase 2 goal now follows the approved BEIR mission, and the article requirement is amended everywhere so future sessions will not start article work.

The shared dataset harness is built and unit-tested: one registry covers Cranfield plus all fourteen public BEIR datasets with tiers and published reference numbers, and one fetch/load/evaluate script family works for any of them.

Both baselines reproduce the published reference numbers: SciFact came in at 0.6906 versus the published 0.665 and NFCorpus at 0.3273 versus the published 0.325, so the new harness is trustworthy and no blocker exists.

Cranfield now runs through the same shared harness as the BEIR datasets and reproduces its Phase 1 numbers exactly, for both the accepted baseline and the refined PRF candidate, so the migration is proven rather than assumed.

Both delegated tasks are complete: the mission updates are recorded, the shared dataset harness is built with Cranfield migrated at exact parity, and the SciFact and NFCorpus baselines reproduce the published reference numbers, so there is no harness bug and no blocker; everything is committed locally and pushing to GitHub is left to you per the no-hosted-push instruction.

Continuing into the next mission step: extending BM25 baselines across the remaining small and mid-size datasets with the sequential load-evaluate-drop pattern; the giant Tier 3 corpora will be scoped and reported rather than started.

One dataset landed far from its published number and was investigated as a suspected harness bug per mission policy: the gap turned out to be a documented evaluation-convention difference (the original benchmark kept a query's own document in its results; modern practice excludes it), confirmed by reproducing both conventions, so the harness stays trustworthy and both numbers are recorded.

Tier 1 is fully baselined and Tier 2 is half done: FiQA, ArguAna, SCIDOCS, TREC-COVID, and Touche all reproduce their published numbers within a few percent, with the two largest corpora already indexed, evaluated, and dropped again to respect the free-tier storage limit.

Quora finished within two and a half percent of its published number across all ten thousand test queries and its index is already dropped again; only the twelve CQADupStack forums remain in the mid-size tier and their download is in progress.

Operator flagged that dataset sizes should be checked from external sources before downloading; header checks now show the CQADupStack archive is by far the biggest in BEIR at five gigabytes while all the supposedly giant Tier 3 downloads are around one gigabyte or less, so the sizes are now recorded in the registry and the almost-finished CQADupStack download will be completed and extracted with a streaming tool.

One forum baseline came out corrupted because the loader script was refactored while a detached run was still using it - a self-inflicted race, not a data problem; that forum will be rerun cleanly and the lesson (never edit scripts under a running detached job) is recorded.

Root cause of the corrupted forum baseline found and fixed: a duplicate copy of the same pipeline was accidentally started after a wrong is-it-dead check and the two copies raced on shared indexes; both are stopped, the contaminated artifacts are purged, and the last five forums are rerunning cleanly under a single supervised job.

The mid-size tier is fully baselined: the twelve-forum duplicate-question benchmark averaged 0.3253 against the published 0.299, within nine percent, closing out ten of the fourteen datasets; the six giant-corpus datasets now run overnight in priority order with the two near-duplicate fact-checking corpora last so they can be cut if needed.

While the biggest corpus indexes, the fast-loop infrastructure for the technique matrix is now generalized: any dataset can cache its top-fifty retrieval pools with document text so reranker experiments run offline; the mid-size datasets will need one extra load-cache-drop pass since their indexes were already dropped after baselining.

The technique matrix has its first cross-domain data points: the Phase 1 rerankers were ported with proven exact fidelity and run offline against the first science dataset, where the techniques that gained up to nine percent on the Phase 1 dataset came out flat - early evidence for domain-conditional rather than universal classification, exactly what the phase is designed to measure.

The technique matrix now covers all five small datasets plus the Phase 1 foundation: neither Phase 1 reranker is trending universal - the pseudo-relevance-feedback reranker improves only half the datasets with its one big gain on the original Phase 1 dataset, and the coverage reranker improves two - so both are heading for domain-conditional portfolio status, and the key open question is whether the embedding hybrid holds up cross-domain.

The lexical technique matrix for the small tier is complete and the verdict is consistent: all three Phase 1 lexical techniques (field-sum, coverage rerank, refined PRF) improve only two or three of six domains each and none clears the universal bar, so the Phase 1 lexical architecture is heading to the domain-conditional portfolio rather than the cross-domain default - the embedding hybrid is now the decisive open question.

The most-cited benchmark in the field reproduced essentially exactly - within one tenth of a percent of the published number across all nearly seven thousand dev queries after indexing almost nine million passages on the free-tier instance; only the two fact-checking corpora remain.

The cross-domain failure analysis sorts all fourteen domains into three regimes - vocabulary mismatch dominating the web and forum datasets, top-of-ranking noise dominating the science and duplicate-question datasets, and broad many-answer queries dominating the deeply-judged corpora - giving each backlog method a specific regime and dataset list to prove itself against.

The technique matrix now has a defensible verdict for the Phase 1 lexical methods across the full Cranfield-plus-small-datasets superset: both the coverage and pseudo-relevance-feedback rerankers are domain-conditional, each helping three of six domains with their wins concentrated on scientific and medical text while pseudo-relevance feedback actively hurts financial and argument retrieval - a real domain signal that makes the portfolio case rather than the default-architecture case.

One dataset baseline is the sweeps only real outlier, coming in about thirteen percent below its published number where every other dataset reproduced within nine percent; this is being investigated rather than accepted, and the next dataset - which shares the exact same document collection - serves as the control that will show whether the indexing is at fault or whether it is a known-hard dataset characteristic.
on: climate-fever baseline 0.1862 vs published 0.213; awaiting fever (same corpus) as control

summary: The outlier investigation resolved: the two fact-checking datasets that share one document collection both come in about thirteen percent below their published numbers, while every other dataset including three other Wikipedia-based collections reproduces within a few percent and the overall average lands exactly on the published baseline average - so the harness is sound and the gap is a localized configuration difference on that one collection, not a general bug. Whether to chase it by re-indexing with search-engine-matched ranking parameters is a methodology decision for the operator, given the mission explicitly says not to optimize benchmark scores for their own sake.
